Apple___Apple_scab.summary: Apple scab is a fungal leaf and fruit disease that spreads quickly in wet weather.
immediate_action: Remove heavily infected leaves and fallen debris from under the tree.
Avoid overhead watering and improve airflow through pruning.
organic_treatment: Use sanitation, pruning, and mulching to reduce spread.
Apply sulfur or other approved organic fungicides if they are labeled for your crop.
chemical_treatment: Use a locally registered fungicide early in the season and follow the label exactly.
prevention: Choose resistant varieties when possible.
Keep the orchard floor clean and dry as much as possible.

Apple___Black_rot.summary: Black rot can spread into fruit, leaves, and twigs and should be managed quickly.
immediate_action: Prune out infected twigs and remove mummified fruit.
Destroy infected plant material instead of composting it.
organic_treatment: Improve airflow and reduce moisture around the canopy.
Use sanitation as the main low-impact control.
chemical_treatment: Apply a registered fungicide as recommended for your region and crop stage.
prevention: Remove fallen fruit and leaves regularly.
Avoid injuries to fruit and branches.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.summary: Cedar apple rust is a fungal disease that cycles between apple trees and nearby junipers or cedars.
immediate_action: Remove heavily infected leaves and fruit if practical.
Inspect nearby junipers or cedars for rust galls.
organic_treatment: Prune for airflow and reduce leaf wetness.
Use resistant apple varieties in future plantings.
chemical_treatment: Use a labeled fungicide preventive program if disease pressure is high.
prevention: Avoid planting apples close to susceptible cedar hosts.
Monitor in spring when infection pressure is highest.

Apple___healthy.summary: The leaf appears healthy. Keep monitoring and maintain good orchard hygiene.
immediate_action: Continue routine scouting for early symptoms.
Keep watering and pruning practices consistent.
organic_treatment: Use mulching, spacing, and sanitation as preventive care.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the tree is healthy.
prevention: Inspect new growth after rainy periods.
Use resistant varieties and clean tools.

Blueberry___healthy.summary: The blueberry leaf appears healthy. Keep monitoring for stress, pests, and fungal spots.
immediate_action: Continue routine plant inspection.
Maintain proper irrigation and soil acidity.
organic_treatment: Use mulch and sanitation to reduce stress.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the plant is healthy.
prevention: Avoid water stress and overcrowding.
Remove debris from around the plant.

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.summary: Powdery mildew is a fungal disease that thrives in crowded canopies and poor airflow.
immediate_action: Remove heavily infected leaves and shoots.
Prune to improve airflow and light penetration.
organic_treatment: Use sulfur or other labeled organic fungicides if suitable.
Keep the canopy open and avoid excess nitrogen.
chemical_treatment: Use a registered fungicide if the disease is spreading.
prevention: Avoid overcrowding and overhead watering.
Monitor new growth closely.

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.summary: The cherry leaf appears healthy. Keep up regular monitoring and pruning.
immediate_action: Inspect new leaves weekly during active growth.
Keep the tree well spaced and ventilated.
organic_treatment: Use sanitation and pruning as routine prevention.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the tree is healthy.
prevention: Remove fallen debris and water at the base only.

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.summary: Gray leaf spot is a fungal disease that reduces photosynthesis and can lower yield.
immediate_action: Check nearby leaves and remove severely infected debris if possible.
Reduce leaf wetness by improving field airflow where practical.
organic_treatment: Rotate crops and manage crop residue carefully.
Use resistant maize varieties when available.
chemical_treatment: Use a labeled fungicide program if local pressure is high.
prevention: Rotate away from maize and avoid continuous corn planting.
Use varieties bred for disease resistance.

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.summary: Common rust is a fungal disease that is often worse in cool, humid weather.
immediate_action: Inspect neighboring plants for early pustules.
Reduce stress and avoid excess canopy moisture.
organic_treatment: Use resistant hybrids when possible.
Keep fields clean of volunteer maize.
chemical_treatment: Use a fungicide only when disease pressure and growth stage justify it.
prevention: Monitor after cool, wet periods.
Rotate crops and destroy volunteer hosts.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.summary: Northern leaf blight can spread fast and cause major leaf loss if conditions stay wet.
immediate_action: Inspect the field and remove severe residue sources where practical.
Reduce canopy wetness and avoid unnecessary irrigation.
organic_treatment: Use resistant hybrids and residue management.
Keep rotation intervals wide.
chemical_treatment: Use a registered fungicide if the infection is progressing in the upper canopy.
prevention: Rotate crops and manage infected residue.
Choose resistant maize hybrids.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.summary: The maize leaf appears healthy. Keep monitoring after rain and during rapid growth.
immediate_action: Continue routine field scouting.
Maintain balanced irrigation and nutrition.
organic_treatment: Use rotation and residue management as preventive care.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the crop is healthy.
prevention: Choose resistant hybrids and manage volunteer maize.

Grape___Black_rot.summary: Black rot can destroy leaves and fruit clusters if it is not controlled early.
immediate_action: Remove infected clusters and mummified berries.
Prune out diseased canes and clean up debris.
organic_treatment: Improve airflow and reduce canopy moisture.
Use sanitation and fruit cluster removal as key controls.
chemical_treatment: Use a registered fungicide program during susceptible growth stages.
prevention: Remove mummies and fallen fruit from the vineyard floor.
Avoid overcrowding and keep pruning sharp.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).summary: Esca is a trunk and wood disease with no simple cure, so infected plants need careful management.
immediate_action: Mark affected vines and remove severely declining wood if advised locally.
Avoid stressing the vine with poor pruning or water stress.
organic_treatment: Focus on sanitation, pruning hygiene, and stress reduction.
Remove infected wood carefully and dispose of it safely.
chemical_treatment: There is no reliable curative spray; follow local expert guidance on vineyard protection.
prevention: Use clean pruning tools and avoid large pruning wounds.
Keep vines vigorous with balanced nutrition and irrigation.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.summary: Leaf blight and leaf spot reduce vine vigor and spread in wet canopies.
immediate_action: Remove heavily infected leaves where practical.
Open the canopy to improve drying after rain.
organic_treatment: Use pruning, spacing, and sanitation to reduce spread.
Keep irrigation off the foliage.
chemical_treatment: Use a locally approved fungicide if the disease pressure is persistent.
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation and overcrowding.
Clean up fallen leaf litter.

Grape___healthy.summary: The grape leaf appears healthy. Keep regular scouting and canopy management.
immediate_action: Continue routine inspection of leaves and clusters.
Keep the canopy open for airflow.
organic_treatment: Use pruning and sanitation as preventive care.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the vine is healthy.
prevention: Avoid water on foliage and maintain clean tools.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).summary: Citrus greening has no cure, so infected trees must be managed aggressively.
immediate_action: Remove or isolate infected trees based on local guidance.
Control insect vectors such as psyllids immediately.
organic_treatment: Use disease-free nursery stock and strict sanitation.
Prune and destroy heavily affected material.
chemical_treatment: Use locally registered vector-control products if recommended by extension services.
prevention: Plant certified healthy trees only.
Monitor regularly for vector insects and symptoms.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.summary: Bacterial spot spreads with rain splash and can weaken leaves and fruit.
immediate_action: Avoid overhead watering and remove heavily infected leaves if possible.
Reduce stress and keep the tree well pruned.
organic_treatment: Use sanitation and canopy airflow as the main controls.
Avoid excessive nitrogen that drives soft growth.
chemical_treatment: Use copper-based or other registered bactericides only if labeled for your crop.
prevention: Choose resistant cultivars when available.
Keep tools clean and avoid working when foliage is wet.

Peach___healthy.summary: The peach leaf appears healthy. Continue monitoring and good orchard hygiene.
immediate_action: Inspect leaves and fruit regularly.
Keep irrigation and pruning balanced.
organic_treatment: Use sanitation and airflow management to prevent disease.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the tree is healthy.
prevention: Avoid wet foliage and keep tools disinfected.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.summary: Bacterial spot can move quickly in peppers and should be managed early.
immediate_action: Remove badly affected leaves if practical.
Avoid touching wet plants and sanitize tools.
organic_treatment: Use crop rotation, mulch, and drip irrigation where possible.
Keep infected debris away from healthy plants.
chemical_treatment: Use a labeled copper product or other locally approved bactericide if recommended.
prevention: Use clean seed or transplants and avoid overhead watering.
Rotate away from peppers and tomatoes.

Pepper,_bell___healthy.summary: The pepper leaf appears healthy. Keep up routine monitoring and sanitation.
immediate_action: Continue regular inspection.
Keep leaves dry and tools clean.
organic_treatment: Use mulch and drip irrigation as preventive care.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the plant is healthy.
prevention: Avoid splashing water and overcrowding.

Potato___Early_blight.summary: Early blight is a common fungal leaf disease that can spread under warm, wet conditions.
immediate_action: Remove heavily infected lower leaves if practical.
Avoid watering the foliage and reduce plant stress.
organic_treatment: Use mulching, rotation, and sanitation to slow spread.
Support the plant with balanced nutrition.
chemical_treatment: Use a labeled fungicide if disease pressure is increasing.
prevention: Rotate crops and clean up old potato debris.
Keep the canopy dry.

Potato___Late_blight.summary: Late blight is a serious disease that can destroy potato foliage and spread fast in cool, wet weather.
immediate_action: Remove or isolate infected plants immediately.
Avoid moving through wet plants and disinfect tools.
organic_treatment: Destroy infected foliage and improve airflow around the crop.
Use strict sanitation and avoid composting diseased material.
chemical_treatment: Use a locally approved fungicide program as soon as the disease is suspected.
prevention: Plant certified seed and avoid overhead watering.
Monitor closely during cool, wet periods.

Potato___healthy.summary: The potato leaf appears healthy. Keep monitoring because blights can develop quickly.
immediate_action: Continue weekly field checks.
Keep plants dry and well spaced.
organic_treatment: Use mulch and rotation to reduce future disease pressure.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the crop is healthy.
prevention: Remove volunteer potatoes and old debris.

Raspberry___healthy.summary: The raspberry leaf appears healthy. Keep monitoring for cane and leaf diseases.
immediate_action: Inspect canes and leaves regularly.
Keep the planting open and manageable.
organic_treatment: Use pruning and sanitation as routine prevention.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the plant is healthy.
prevention: Remove old fruiting canes and debris.

Soybean___healthy.summary: The soybean leaf appears healthy. Keep scouting for rust, blight, and insect stress.
immediate_action: Continue routine scouting.
Maintain balanced nutrition and irrigation.
organic_treatment: Use rotation and residue management to reduce disease pressure.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the crop is healthy.
prevention: Watch for sudden weather-driven disease outbreaks.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.summary: Powdery mildew can cover squash leaves quickly, especially in crowded plantings.
immediate_action: Remove heavily infected leaves where practical.
Improve airflow around the plants.
organic_treatment: Use sulfur or other labeled organic fungicides if suitable.
Avoid excess nitrogen and overhead watering.
chemical_treatment: Use a registered fungicide if the disease is spreading.
prevention: Space plants well and keep foliage dry.
Monitor weekly during warm weather.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.summary: Leaf scorch reduces strawberry vigor and can spread in humid conditions.
immediate_action: Remove heavily damaged leaves if practical.
Keep plants well spaced and reduce humidity around the canopy.
organic_treatment: Use mulch, sanitation, and drip irrigation where possible.
chemical_treatment: Use a locally approved fungicide if the infection is progressing.
prevention: Avoid overhead watering and overcrowding.
Keep planting beds clean.

Strawberry___healthy.summary: The strawberry leaf appears healthy. Keep scouting regularly and maintain clean beds.
immediate_action: Continue routine inspection.
Remove dead leaves and runners as needed.
organic_treatment: Use mulch and drip irrigation to reduce disease pressure.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the plant is healthy.
prevention: Keep beds clean and avoid splashing water.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.summary: Bacterial spot spreads with water splash and can damage both foliage and fruit.
immediate_action: Remove the worst affected leaves and isolate nearby plants if possible.
Avoid touching wet plants and sanitize tools.
organic_treatment: Use mulch and drip irrigation to reduce splash.
Keep the canopy open and avoid excess nitrogen.
chemical_treatment: Use a labeled copper-based product or other locally approved bactericide if recommended.
prevention: Rotate away from tomatoes and peppers.
Use clean seed and transplants.

Tomato___Early_blight.summary: Early blight causes leaf spots and leaf drop, especially in warm and humid conditions.
immediate_action: Remove lower infected leaves where practical.
Water at the base and keep foliage dry.
organic_treatment: Mulch around plants and prune for airflow.
Keep crop residue away from healthy plants.
chemical_treatment: Use a registered fungicide if disease spread is increasing.
prevention: Rotate crops and clean up old tomato debris.
Avoid overcrowding.

Tomato___Late_blight.summary: Late blight is a rapid-spreading disease that needs immediate attention.
immediate_action: Remove infected plants or leaves immediately if local guidance allows.
Avoid watering foliage and move healthy plants away from infected material.
organic_treatment: Destroy infected debris and improve airflow.
Use strict sanitation and keep plants dry.
chemical_treatment: Use a locally approved fungicide program as soon as late blight is suspected.
prevention: Monitor closely during cool, wet weather.
Plant resistant varieties if available.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.summary: Leaf mold grows in humid, poorly ventilated conditions and can weaken tomato plants.
immediate_action: Improve airflow and reduce humidity around the plants.
Remove heavily infected foliage if practical.
organic_treatment: Use spacing, pruning, and drip irrigation.
Keep greenhouse or tunnel humidity under control.
chemical_treatment: Use a labeled fungicide if the disease is established and spreading.
prevention: Avoid overhead watering and overcrowding.
Ventilate protected growing areas well.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.summary: Septoria leaf spot causes many small spots and defoliation if not managed.
immediate_action: Remove the lowest infected leaves carefully.
Keep water off the foliage and mulch the soil.
organic_treatment: Use mulch, spacing, and sanitation to reduce splash spread.
Prune lower leaves for better airflow.
chemical_treatment: Use a registered fungicide if disease is expanding.
prevention: Rotate crops and avoid working wet plants.
Remove fallen leaf debris promptly.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.summary: Spider mites are pests rather than a fungus, and they can quickly stress tomato leaves.
immediate_action: Check the undersides of leaves and isolate heavily infested plants if possible.
Wash the plant gently with water where appropriate.
organic_treatment: Encourage beneficial insects and keep dust down.
Use horticultural soap or oil only if labeled for your crop.
chemical_treatment: Use a registered miticide only when infestation is significant and label directions allow it.
prevention: Keep plants well watered and avoid dusty, stressed conditions.
Inspect the underside of leaves regularly.

Tomato___Target_Spot.summary: Target spot causes leaf lesions and can reduce plant vigor under warm, humid conditions.
immediate_action: Remove heavily infected leaves if practical.
Increase airflow and keep foliage dry.
organic_treatment: Use mulch, sanitation, and pruning to lower disease pressure.
chemical_treatment: Use a labeled fungicide if the disease is spreading.
prevention: Rotate crops and keep plant debris out of the bed.
Avoid overcrowding.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.summary: This virus has no cure, so focus on removing infected plants and controlling whiteflies.
immediate_action: Remove infected plants to limit spread.
Control whiteflies and other local vectors immediately.
organic_treatment: Use reflective mulch, insect netting, and sanitation where practical.
Plant resistant varieties when available.
chemical_treatment: Use locally registered vector-control products only if advised by local experts.
prevention: Start with healthy transplants and inspect them carefully.
Keep weeds and alternate hosts under control.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.summary: Tomato mosaic virus has no cure and can spread through tools, hands, and plant material.
immediate_action: Remove infected plants and keep them away from healthy ones.
Disinfect tools and wash hands after handling plants.
organic_treatment: Use strict sanitation and resistant varieties.
Avoid tobacco products near the crop if that is a known local risk.
chemical_treatment: There is no curative chemical treatment; focus on sanitation and resistant plants.
prevention: Use clean seed, clean tools, and resistant cultivars.
Do not save seed from infected plants.

Tomato___healthy.summary: The tomato leaf appears healthy. Keep monitoring because tomatoes can develop disease quickly.
immediate_action: Continue weekly leaf checks.
Keep the plant well spaced and watered at the base.
organic_treatment: Use mulch and pruning to keep leaves off the soil.
chemical_treatment: No treatment is needed when the plant is healthy.
prevention: Rotate crops and sanitize tools regularly.
